result.meeting_context: The Council Finance Committee of Mountain View met virtually to review the FY2021–22 Annual Comprehensive Financial Report (ACFR) and external audit results, ask questions, and make a recommendation to the City Council. Discussion also covered equipment fund balances, tracking community benefit revenues, document updates for clarity/usability, and potential state budget impacts on city programs.

topics.topic: FY2022 ACFR Audit Results and Highlights

points.speaker: Banjamin Lau
points.text: MGO completed the City’s FY2022 financial statement audit and reported a clean opinion with an emphasis of matter for GASB 87 lease implementation. Lau also stated “modified opinions” before clarifying “clean,” creating a wording inconsistency. No internal control findings or unrecorded misstatements were identified.
points.source: Banjamin Lau : In summary, we have completed our audit ... have issued A modified opinions ... In other words, it's a clean opinion ... We did not identify any findings on internal control over financial reporting for this year.

points.speaker: Banjamin Lau
points.text: Implementation of GASB 87 resulted in recording about $800 million of lease receivables and offsetting deferred inflows on the balance sheet, primarily from large tech-related leases. Auditors examined these balances in detail and deemed the amounts reasonably stated.
points.source: Banjamin Lau : ...your balance sheet itself has recorded a lease receivable of roughly about 800 million with an offsetting deferred inflow of resources... mainly related to a few leases for one of your gigantic tech firm...

points.speaker: Banjamin Lau
points.text: The Single Audit over federal awards, including ARPA-related activity, has not yet been completed, but MGO expects to meet the statutory deadline of March 31, 2023 for submission.
points.source: Banjamin Lau : ...regarding single audit ... we have not audited yet, but we expect to meet the statutory deadline of March 31st, 2023.

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: MGO has served as the City’s external auditor for seven years, and the City Charter (Section 1106) requires an independent CPA to perform the annual audit. A Council consent item to accept the ACFR and related audit reports is scheduled for December 13, pending this committee’s recommendation.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : Mgos been the external auditor for the city for the last seven years now... city charter Section 11 O Six that specifies that an independent CPA needs to perform this function... a consent item ... scheduled for the December 13th agenda ... pending a recommendation from this committee today.

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: The General Operating Fund finished fiscal year 2022 with an operating balance of approximately $17.5 million, as noted in the staff introduction to the ACFR and audit results.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : ...the general operating fund finished the year, fiscal year 2022 with a 17 and a half million dollar operating balance.

topics.topic: Equipment Replacement Fund Balance and Charges

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: No significant one-time equipment replacement expenditures like the recent fire apparatus purchase are anticipated; most fleet replacements occur item-by-item. Partial payments for the fire fleet are being made over one to two years, so some committed funds remain in the current balance.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : ...no, we don't have anything that's of that nature in terms of the significant one time expenditure... we have made a partial payment, but that's going to be overtime ... one and two years ... some of that is still there and it's reflected in that $26 million...

points.speaker: Banjamin Lau
points.text: The $26 million equipment maintenance and replacement fund balance shown as “cash and investments” reflects the fund’s share of the City’s pooled cash and investments, not a separate account. The pooling approach maximizes returns while maintaining liquidity to meet operational needs.
points.source: Banjamin Lau : ...the city operate as a whole has a pool of cash... the dollar amount that you're seeing is the internal service funds respective portion of the whole pool... one of the criterias is always about liquidity.

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: Charges are set to amortize replacement costs (including inflation) over asset lives, aiming for 100% funding without overcollecting. Longer-than-expected vehicle service lives can slow spending and increase balances; staff acknowledged reviewing funding levels and potential charge adjustments could be appropriate.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : ...those are amortized at an expected replacement value... we typically don't over fund... we may not spend as quickly as we initially project... maybe we can lower charges ... we ... can look at and ... get back to the committee with an update.

topics.topic: Community Benefit Revenues Tracking and Reporting

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: Community benefit contributions are tracked in detail within the City’s financial system subledgers, but the ACFR aggregates them with other developer fees and does not separately report them. Staff can produce tracking from internal ledgers even though the ACFR does not breakout those amounts.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : ...we do have a mechanism for tracking those community benefit funds... it's done within the city's financial system ledgers... it's not something that is reported or shown within this document, though the ACT firm.

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: Some community benefits are provided directly by developers (e.g., below-market rents, parking, infrastructure) and do not flow through the City; these are not recorded on City ledgers, so external reports may present an incomplete picture of total benefits delivered.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : ...to the extent the city's not receiving the cash payment... that would not get recorded on our on our ledger's... we may have an incomplete picture from that standpoint.

topics.topic: Top Employers Schedule Clarification and Context

points.speaker: Banjamin Lau
points.text: The ACFR’s top employers schedule lists only the current top ten, with a cutoff around 500 employees; an employer like LinkedIn may not appear if below that threshold, not necessarily indicating zero employees. The schedule also shows the top ten from ten years ago for historical comparison.
points.source: Banjamin Lau : ...this schedule only showed us a top 10 ... the cut off for this year is at 500... if they have 499, it doesn't get to this list. / Jesse Takahashi : ...it also shows what the top ten were up 10 years ago.

points.speaker: Grace Zheng
points.text: A decrease in public safety expenses reflected in the ACFR relates to GASB 68 pension accounting: significant CalPERS investment income reduced entity-wide pension expense, and the public safety pension plan’s impact appears specifically in the public safety category.
points.source: Grace Zheng : ...including Gatsby 68 for Cowper's pension... significant investment income earning... positive impact reduce our entity wide expenditure... the public safety plan hits the public safety category only.

topics.topic: ACFR Document Updates and Formatting Improvements

points.speaker: Lisa Matichak
points.text: A request was made to replace “Mountain View citizens” with “Mountain View residents” and to add the Public Safety Advisory Board in the ACFR exhibits. Staff acknowledged the discrepancy and agreed to correct these references in the ACFR.
points.source: Lisa Matichak : ...update this?... Instead of having Mountain View citizens, it would be Mountain View residents... add Public Safety Advisory Board. / Jesse Takahashi : ...we need to make that change also here in that for... We'll get that corrected.

points.speaker: Jesse Takahashi
points.text: Staff agreed to reformat the ACFR for better electronic readability, responding to feedback that the current print-oriented layout makes side-by-side comparisons difficult in digital form.
points.source: Jesse Takahashi : Thank you for the comments and we'll definitely get to work on trying to reformat for electronic age.

topics.topic: Recommendation to Accept ACFR and Reports

points.speaker: Lucas Ramirez
points.text: The committee voted unanimously to recommend that the City Council accept the FY2021–22 ACFR and the auditor’s reports, as a consent item slated for the December 13 Council agenda.
points.source: Victoria labrador : Unanimous. / Lucas Ramirez : I'm happy to to move the staff recommendation... recommend adoption of the comprehensive financial report to council.

points.speaker: Sally Lieber
points.text: The Chair requested staff provide the Council with information on how the State’s projected budget shortfalls (approximately $25 billion, potentially $50 billion next year) could affect City programs that currently rely on anticipated state funding.
points.source: Sally Lieber : ...the legislative analysts report was concerning of $25 billion hole ... growing to a potential $50 billion ... information for council on how that will impact some of the programs that we've made a commitment to would be great.

points.speaker: Sally Lieber
points.text: Committee members thanked the external auditor and City finance staff for preparing the ACFR and audit, and acknowledged Jesse Takahashi’s service as this was noted as his last meeting with the committee, before adjourning.
points.source: Lucas Ramirez : ...thank you very much for your service to the city... it's been a pleasure working with you... / Sally Lieber : ...really appreciate everything that you've done to bring this to us.

decisions.text: Voted unanimously to recommend City Council acceptance of the FY2021–22 ACFR and auditor’s reports.
decisions.source: Victoria labrador : Unanimous. / Lucas Ramirez : ...move the staff recommendation... recommend adoption of the comprehensive financial report to council.

decisions.text: Complete and submit the Single Audit by the March 31, 2023 statutory deadline.
decisions.source: Banjamin Lau : ...we have not audited yet, but we expect to meet the statutory deadline of March 31st, 2023.

decisions.text: Review Equipment Maintenance and Replacement Fund funding levels and charges and report back to the committee with potential rate adjustments to maintain reasonable balances.
decisions.source: Jesse Takahashi : ...we certainly can look at and you know get get back to the committee with an update... if there's some adjustment to the rates that we can look at...

decisions.text: Update ACFR exhibits to replace “citizens” with “residents” and include the Public Safety Advisory Board.
decisions.source: Jesse Takahashi : ...we need to make that change also here in that for... We'll get that corrected.

decisions.text: Reformat the ACFR for improved readability in electronic format.
decisions.source: Jesse Takahashi : ...we'll definitely get to work on trying to reformat for electronic age.
